meanings.definition: A false or assumed name used in place of one's real name.
examples: The spy traveled under an alias to avoid detection.

meanings.definition: Used to indicate that a named person is also known by another name.
examples: The author George Eliot, alias Mary Ann Evans, wrote under a pen name.

meanings.definition: A love affair, especially a secret or illicit one.
examples: Rumors of the count's amour spread quickly through the court.

meanings.definition: Relating to, secreting, or resembling fat or oil.
examples: The sebaceous glands in the skin produce an oily substance called sebum.

meanings.definition: To light up or supply with light.
examples: Floodlights illuminated the stadium for the night game.

meanings.definition: To make something clearer or easier to understand.
examples: Her lecture illuminated several difficult points in the theory.

meanings.definition: The act of putting things in order, or the resulting orderly state.
examples: The arrangement of the books by subject made them easy to find.

meanings.definition: A plan or agreement made in preparation for something.
examples: We made arrangements for the guests to be picked up at the airport.

meanings.definition: To identify a disease or problem by examining its signs and symptoms.
examples: The doctor was able to diagnose the illness from a simple blood test.

meanings.definition: A person or thing firmly established or expected to remain permanently in place.
examples: After thirty years, the old shopkeeper had become a fixture in the neighborhood.

meanings.definition: A piece of equipment fixed in position as a permanent part of a building.
examples: The new owners replaced all the lighting fixtures in the house.

meanings.definition: Not helpful, encouraging, or likely to lead to a good outcome; adverse.
examples: Unfavorable weather forced the expedition to turn back.

meanings.definition: The quality or state of giving off or radiating light; brightness.
examples: Astronomers measure the luminosity of distant stars to estimate their size.

meanings.definition: Relating to or placed in the region just beneath the skin.
examples: The nurse gave the vaccine with a hypodermic needle.

meanings.definition: To come or go in without permission or welcome.
examples: I didn't mean to intrude on your private conversation.
Reporters intruded into the family's home during their grief.

meanings.definition: To force or impose something unwelcome on someone.
examples: She tried not to intrude her own opinions on the group.

meanings.definition: A short passage taken from a longer piece of writing, music, or film.
examples: The teacher read an excerpt from the novel aloud to the class.
The magazine printed an excerpt of the author's new memoir.

meanings.definition: A very small amount or particle of something.
examples: She gave what little she could, just a mite toward the cause.
He was not a mite worried about the exam.

meanings.definition: A tiny arachnid, often parasitic, related to ticks.
examples: Dust mites are a common cause of indoor allergies.

meanings.definition: A weight hung on a line to check whether something is perfectly vertical.
examples: The builder dropped a plumb to make sure the wall was straight.

meanings.definition: To measure the depth of, or to explore and understand something thoroughly.
examples: Scientists tried to plumb the depths of the ocean trench.
She struggled to plumb the meaning of the cryptic poem.

meanings.definition: Exactly or completely (informal).
examples: The arrow landed plumb in the center of the target.

meanings.definition: A short concluding section at the end of a story, play, or poem.
examples: The epilogue revealed what became of the characters years later.
An actor stepped forward to deliver the play's epilogue.

meanings.definition: A large mass of snow, ice, and rock sliding rapidly down a mountainside.
examples: The skiers were warned that an avalanche could break loose at any moment.
An avalanche buried the mountain road under tons of snow.

meanings.definition: A sudden, overwhelming arrival of a great quantity of things.
examples: The announcement triggered an avalanche of angry emails.

meanings.definition: A tomb or burial chamber, especially one cut in rock or built of stone.
examples: The knight was laid to rest in a marble sepulcher.
Pilgrims visited the ancient sepulcher carved into the hillside.

meanings.definition: An official book listing medicinal drugs along with their formulas and instructions for preparation.
examples: The new remedy was added to the national pharmacopoeia.
Pharmacists once relied on the pharmacopoeia to mix prescriptions by hand.

meanings.definition: A prediction of what will happen in the future, often presented as divinely inspired.
examples: The ancient prophecy foretold the fall of the kingdom.
Her gloomy prophecy about the economy turned out to be wrong.

meanings.definition: An extra benefit or privilege gained from a job in addition to one's pay.
examples: A company car was one of the perquisites of his new position.
Free travel is a perquisite that comes with the job.

meanings.definition: The science that studies the earth's physical structure, rocks, and the processes that shape it.
examples: She majored in geology to study volcanoes and earthquakes.
The geology of the canyon reveals millions of years of history.

meanings.definition: Having a full, curvaceous, and attractive physical form.
examples: The painting depicted a voluptuous figure draped in silk.

meanings.definition: Giving rich sensory pleasure; luxurious.
examples: They sank into the voluptuous comfort of the velvet chairs.
The dessert had a voluptuous, creamy texture.

meanings.definition: A thick, semi-liquid mixture of ingredients beaten together for cooking, such as for cakes or pancakes.
examples: She poured the pancake batter onto the hot griddle.
The cake batter was too runny, so he added more flour.

meanings.definition: To strike repeatedly with heavy blows.
examples: Waves battered the coastline during the storm.

meanings.definition: The player whose turn it is to hit the ball in baseball or cricket.
examples: The batter swung hard and hit a home run.

meanings.definition: A square piece of cloth worn as a covering for the head or neck.
examples: She tied a bright kerchief over her hair to keep off the dust.
He wore a red kerchief knotted around his neck.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characteristic of flowing streams or rivers.
examples: The valley showed fluential features carved by ancient rivers.
Geologists noted the fluential patterns in the sediment.

meanings.definition: To help bring about or contribute to a particular result.
examples: Regular exercise can conduce to better overall health.
A quiet room conduces to focused study.

meanings.definition: The blocking of a blood vessel by a clot, air bubble, or other obstruction carried in the bloodstream.
examples: The patient suffered an embolism that cut off blood flow to the lung.
Doctors acted quickly to dissolve the embolism.

meanings.definition: Friendly, easy to talk to, and pleasant in manner.
examples: The affable host made every guest feel welcome.
Despite his fame, he remained affable and approachable.

meanings.definition: A person who does not believe in a particular religion or in God.
examples: The crusaders set out to wage war against those they called infidels.
He was branded an infidel for rejecting the church's teachings.

meanings.definition: A word, especially one considered for its sound rather than its meaning.
examples: The poet chose each vocable for its musical quality.
Children often delight in repeating a funny vocable.

meanings.definition: A result or consequence produced by a cause or action.
examples: The new policy had an immediate effect on sales.
Years of pollution had a damaging effect on the river.

meanings.definition: To bring about or cause something to happen.
examples: The committee worked to effect real change in the community.

meanings.definition: To destroy or remove something completely so that no trace remains.
examples: The flood obliterated the small village within minutes.
Time had obliterated the inscription on the old gravestone.

meanings.definition: Pale, especially because of poor health or strong emotion.
examples: Her pallid face showed how ill she truly was.
He looked pallid and shaken after the accident.

meanings.definition: Containing contaminating or unwanted substances; not pure.
examples: The water was impure and unsafe to drink.
Impure metal had to be refined before use.

meanings.definition: Morally wrong or unchaste.
examples: The strict elders condemned what they saw as impure thoughts.

meanings.definition: A person who spends money or resources recklessly and wastefully.
examples: The young prodigal squandered his entire inheritance in a year.

meanings.definition: Spending or giving lavishly and often wastefully.
examples: He was prodigal with his money but generous to his friends.
The garden was prodigal with color all summer long.

meanings.definition: The forward part of a ship's upper deck, traditionally housing the crew's quarters.
examples: The sailors gathered in the forecastle to wait out the storm.
Spray washed over the forecastle as the bow plunged into the waves.

meanings.definition: Located in the interior of a country, away from the sea or coast.
examples: The inland towns rarely saw the heavy fog of the coast.
They moved to an inland city far from the ocean.

meanings.definition: Toward or into the interior, away from the coast.
examples: The river flows many miles inland before reaching the lake.

meanings.definition: To calm or satisfy someone, especially by giving in to their demands.
examples: They offered gifts to appease the angry crowd.
Nothing he said could appease her wounded feelings.

meanings.definition: A determined and often sudden attack upon an enemy.
examples: The defenders braced for the enemy's fierce onset.

meanings.definition: The beginning or start of something, often unpleasant.
examples: At the onset of winter, the birds flew south.
Early treatment can slow the onset of the disease.

meanings.definition: The act of condemning, outlawing, or forbidding something.
examples: The proscription of the old customs angered many villagers.
The new law brought the proscription of several political groups.

meanings.definition: A coarse, sturdy, heavy work shoe or ankle boot.
examples: The farmer pulled on his muddy brogans before heading to the field.
Each laborer was issued a pair of stiff leather brogans.

meanings.definition: A person skilled at planning and directing actions to achieve a goal.
examples: The general was a brilliant tactician who outwitted larger armies.
As a tactician, she always anticipated her opponent's next move.

meanings.definition: A person's face or facial expression.
examples: A smile slowly spread across his weathered visage.
Her stern visage softened when she saw the children.

meanings.definition: Relating to the enforcing of rules, order, or punishment for misconduct.
examples: The student faced disciplinary action for cheating.
The company held a disciplinary hearing over the incident.

meanings.definition: Relating to a particular branch of knowledge or field of study.
examples: The project required disciplinary expertise in both biology and chemistry.

meanings.definition: A person who supervises and directs the work of others.
examples: The overseer made sure every worker met the daily quota.
As overseer of the estate, she managed all the laborers.

meanings.definition: Expressing or full of praise.
examples: The critic wrote a laudatory review of the new play.
Her speech was so laudatory that the honoree blushed.

meanings.definition: The offspring or descendants of a person, animal, or plant.
examples: The prize bull sired a long line of healthy progeny.
His progeny went on to build successful careers of their own.

meanings.definition: Guilty of an offense or sin; doing wrong.
examples: The peccant clerk was dismissed for falsifying records.
She confessed her peccant behavior to the priest.

meanings.definition: The desire to harm others; deep ill will or spite.
examples: His eyes gleamed with malevolence as he plotted revenge.
There was real malevolence behind her seemingly kind words.

meanings.definition: To catch in or as if in a trap; to entangle.
examples: The hunters used nets to ensnare the wild birds.
She was ensnared by his charm before she realized his true intentions.

meanings.definition: Failing to make the best use of time, resources, or effort; not effective.
examples: The old heating system was noisy and highly inefficient.
Their inefficient process wasted hours every day.

meanings.definition: A woman who is the head of a convent of nuns.
examples: The abbess governed the convent with quiet authority.
Young novices sought the abbess's blessing each morning.

meanings.definition: Beyond what is normal or natural; extraordinary or seemingly supernatural.
examples: She solved the puzzle with preternatural speed.
An eerie, preternatural silence settled over the forest.

meanings.definition: Able to be done, achieved, or to exist; within the reach of one's power or circumstances.
examples: It is possible to finish the project by Friday if we hurry.
With practice, almost anything is possible.

meanings.definition: That may be true or may happen but is not certain.
examples: Rain is possible later this afternoon.

meanings.definition: Absolutely necessary and impossible to do without.
examples: A good map was indispensable on the long hike.
She quickly became indispensable to the entire team.

meanings.definition: A person regarded as primitive or uncivilized (now considered offensive).
examples: Early explorers wrongly described the islanders as savages.

meanings.definition: Fierce, violent, or uncontrolled.
examples: The savage storm tore roofs off the houses.
He launched a savage attack on his critics.

meanings.definition: To make someone feel anxious or uneasy; to disturb their peace of mind.
examples: The strange noises in the night disquieted the campers.
Rumors of layoffs disquieted the entire office.

meanings.definition: Readily changing in appearance, character, or behavior.
examples: The actor had a chameleon talent for slipping into any role.
Her chameleon moods made her hard to predict.

meanings.definition: To turn around a central point or axis.
examples: The Earth rotates once every twenty-four hours.
She rotated the dial until it clicked into place.

meanings.definition: To take turns or pass through a regular sequence of positions or duties.
examples: The nurses rotate between the day and night shifts each week.

meanings.definition: To spoil or damage the appearance of someone or something.
examples: The fire disfigured the once-beautiful facade of the building.
A deep scar disfigured his cheek.

meanings.definition: A person who takes great pleasure in fine food and drink.
examples: As a true epicure, he savored every course of the meal.
The restaurant attracted epicures from across the country.

meanings.definition: A piece of clothing worn beneath one's outer clothes.
examples: She packed warm undergarments for the winter trip.
The thin shirt revealed the undergarment beneath it.

meanings.definition: A lack of ability, power, or capacity to do something.
examples: His incapacity to manage money left him deep in debt.
An illness caused her temporary incapacity to work.

meanings.definition: A person who gives money, goods, or something such as blood or an organ.
examples: A generous donor funded the new library wing.
The patient was waiting for a kidney from a matching donor.

meanings.definition: Occurring or located within a cell of the body.
examples: The virus replicates within the intracellular fluid.
Researchers studied the intracellular structures under a microscope.

meanings.definition: Deliberately behaving in an unreasonable or contrary way, especially against what is expected or right.
examples: He took a perverse pleasure in proving everyone wrong.
It would be perverse to ignore such clear evidence.

meanings.definition: A person's manner of walking or moving on foot.
examples: You could recognize him from a distance by his rolling gait.
An injury left her with an uneven gait.

meanings.definition: To make things equal or uniform in size, amount, or status.
examples: The new policy aims to equalize pay between men and women.
Adding water helped equalize the pressure in the tank.

meanings.definition: In the dark or in a way that involves darkness or blindness (literary).
examples: The travelers wandered darkling through the unlit forest.
He groped darkling for the door in the windowless room.

meanings.definition: To act against something with equal force, balancing or offsetting it.
examples: Higher wages may countervail the rising cost of living.
No argument could countervail the weight of the evidence.

meanings.definition: Kindness, gentleness, and a gracious disposition or manner.
examples: The old doctor treated his patients with great benignity.
Her benignity put even the most nervous visitors at ease.

meanings.definition: To reduce costs or spending, especially in difficult times.
examples: The company had to retrench after a year of heavy losses.
Families often retrench when the economy weakens.

meanings.definition: To do something one considers beneath one's dignity; to condescend.
examples: She would not even deign to reply to his message.
The star finally deigned to sign a few autographs.

meanings.definition: To treat someone with excessive care, indulgence, or protection.
examples: His parents coddle him and never let him face any hardship.
Don't coddle the new recruits; let them learn on their own.

meanings.definition: To cook something, especially eggs, gently in water just below boiling.
examples: She coddled the eggs slowly for a soft, creamy texture.

meanings.definition: Possessing or showing great wisdom and good judgment.
examples: The sapient old scholar offered advice that proved invaluable.
Her sapient remarks calmed the heated debate.

meanings.definition: Showing cleverness, originality, or inventiveness.
examples: She came up with an ingenious solution to the problem.
The ingenious design folds flat for easy storage.

meanings.definition: A person who acts unselfishly for the welfare of others.
examples: A true altruist, she donated most of her fortune to charity.
Altruists volunteer their time without expecting any reward.

meanings.definition: Having six feet or legs, as insects do.
examples: Insects are hexapod creatures with three pairs of legs.
The scientist studied the hexapod anatomy of the beetle.

meanings.definition: Existing only in the imagination; not real.
examples: The lonely child invented an imaginary friend.
His fears turned out to be entirely imaginary.

meanings.definition: A young man, especially a slim or boyish one.
examples: He was just a stripling when he first went to sea.
The veteran soldier had once been a frightened stripling.

meanings.definition: A person who earnestly seeks or aims at something, such as advancement, honor, or a position.
examples: She was one of several aspirants for the leadership of the party.
The young aspirant trained for years hoping to make the Olympic team.

meanings.definition: The state of being kept or restricted within limits or boundaries.
examples: The prisoner endured years of solitary confinement.
After surgery, weeks of confinement to bed left him restless.

meanings.definition: Closely relevant and appropriate to the matter at hand.
examples: Her question was germane to the discussion and sharpened everyone's focus.
Please keep your remarks germane to the topic we are debating.

meanings.definition: A temporary lessening or disappearance of the symptoms of a disease.
examples: After treatment, her cancer went into remission.
The patient enjoyed a long remission before the illness returned.

meanings.definition: The cancellation of a debt, penalty, or punishment.
examples: The judge granted a remission of part of his sentence for good behavior.

meanings.definition: Characterized by or involving rivalry between people or groups striving for the same goal.
examples: The two firms operate in a highly competitive market.
He has a competitive streak and hates to lose at anything.

meanings.definition: The scientific study of past human life and culture through the examination of physical remains and artifacts.
examples: Her interest in archaeology took her to excavation sites across Egypt.
Advances in archaeology have revealed how ancient cities were built.

meanings.definition: Offensively self-assertive and conceited.
examples: The bumptious new manager irritated everyone with his constant boasting.
She found his bumptious manner exhausting at the meeting.

meanings.definition: To agree to a request, proposal, or demand.
examples: The board finally acceded to the workers' demands for higher pay.
He acceded to her wishes and canceled the trip.

meanings.definition: To assume an office or position, especially the throne.
examples: She acceded to the throne after her father's death.

meanings.definition: Important, well-known, or standing out so as to be readily noticed.
examples: She is a prominent figure in the world of science.
A prominent sign warned drivers of the sharp bend ahead.

meanings.definition: A word consisting of only one syllable.
examples: "Cat" and "dog" are monosyllables.
He answered every question in a single monosyllable.

meanings.definition: To entertain or delight someone, especially with talk, stories, or lavish hospitality.
examples: He regaled us with tales of his travels through Asia.
Our host regaled the guests with a magnificent feast.

meanings.definition: Passive acceptance or agreement without protest.
examples: His silence was taken as acquiescence to the plan.
Her acquiescence surprised those who expected an argument.

meanings.definition: To prove or confirm that something is true, accurate, or genuine.
examples: Please verify your email address before continuing.
Investigators worked to verify the witness's account.

meanings.definition: A word, idiom, or phrase characteristic of British English usage.
examples: "Lorry" for truck is a well-known Briticism.
The editor flagged several Briticisms for the American edition.

meanings.definition: A person who makes an excessive or tedious display of learning or insists on petty rules.
examples: Only a pedant would correct your grammar at a birthday party.
The professor was a pedant who fussed over every comma.

meanings.definition: Unable to be felt by touch.
examples: A fine, impalpable dust covered every surface.
The mist was so thin it seemed almost impalpable.

meanings.definition: Difficult to grasp or comprehend by the mind.
examples: There was an impalpable tension in the room that no one could name.

meanings.definition: Serving as a desirable model; outstandingly good.
examples: Her exemplary conduct earned her the respect of her peers.
The team showed exemplary teamwork throughout the season.

meanings.definition: Capable of being transmitted from one person to another, as a disease.
examples: The flu is highly contagious in crowded places.
He stayed home while his illness was still contagious.

meanings.definition: Tending to spread quickly from one person to others, as an emotion or mood.
examples: Her enthusiasm was contagious, and soon the whole class joined in.

meanings.definition: A slight hint, suggestion, or vague idea about something.
examples: I had no inkling that they were planning a surprise party.
She gave us an inkling of her intentions during the meeting.

meanings.definition: To come to possess or get something as one's own.
examples: The museum recently acquired a rare painting.
Over the years she acquired a deep knowledge of the law.

meanings.definition: To pass slowly through a porous material or filter.
examples: Rainwater percolates down through the soil to the aquifer.
Let the coffee percolate for a few minutes before serving.

meanings.definition: To spread or develop gradually.
examples: News of the merger began to percolate through the office.

meanings.definition: A light, colorless, volatile, flammable liquid distilled from petroleum and used as a solvent and in fuels.
examples: The lab used naphtha to dissolve the oily residue.
Naphtha is a key ingredient in many industrial paints.

meanings.definition: A very small or inadequate amount of money or allowance.
examples: He worked long hours for a mere pittance.
The pension they offered was a pittance compared to his needs.

meanings.definition: A person who attaches themselves to others for personal advantage; a parasite.
examples: The celebrity was surrounded by hangers-on hoping for favors.
Once his money ran out, every hanger-on disappeared.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that defends or guards someone or something from harm.
examples: He saw himself as the protector of his younger siblings.
A sturdy case acts as a protector for the fragile device.

meanings.definition: Having sharp and penetrating perception or discernment.
examples: Her acute observation caught the error everyone else missed.
He has an acute sense of smell.

meanings.definition: (Of a bad situation or illness) severe and intense, often coming on suddenly.
examples: The region faces an acute shortage of clean water.
She was rushed to hospital with acute appendicitis.

meanings.definition: A person who is habitually lazy or slow-moving.
examples: Don't be a sluggard; there's work to be done.
The sluggard slept while everyone else prepared for the trip.

meanings.definition: Extremely useful or precious; beyond measure in value.
examples: Her advice proved invaluable during the crisis.
The old maps were an invaluable resource for the historians.

meanings.definition: Not allowing light to pass through; not transparent.
examples: The bathroom window was made of opaque glass.
She painted the jar with an opaque coat so the contents were hidden.

meanings.definition: Hard to understand or unclear.
examples: The instructions were so opaque that no one could follow them.

meanings.definition: Serving as a preliminary or beginning; intended to introduce something.
examples: The professor gave a brief introductory lecture on the course.
An introductory offer let new members join at half price.

meanings.definition: To set up or settle in a camp.
examples: The troops encamped on the hillside for the night.
We encamped beside the river for three days.

meanings.definition: A condition or stipulation attached to an agreement, will, or contract.
examples: He agreed to the deal with the proviso that he could leave at any time.
The contract included a proviso about late payments.

meanings.definition: Intense dislike, hatred, or fear of England or things English.
examples: His Anglophobia made him refuse anything imported from Britain.
The pamphlet was full of crude Anglophobia.

meanings.definition: To comment on or reflect upon moral questions, often in a self-righteous way.
examples: He loves to moralize about other people's spending habits.
She moralized at length about the dangers of laziness.

meanings.definition: To make or render virtuous or moral.
examples: The reformers hoped to moralize the youth of the city.

meanings.definition: A small group within a larger body, united by a common purpose and often in opposition to others.
examples: A rebellious faction split off from the main party.
Rival factions struggled for control of the council.

meanings.definition: Giving sufficient grounds for a lawsuit or legal action.
examples: His false claims were actionable as slander.
The lawyer said the breach of contract was clearly actionable.

meanings.definition: Able to be acted on; providing a basis for practical action.
examples: The report offered actionable advice for improving sales.

meanings.definition: To act upon or produce an effect or change in something.
examples: The drought severely affected the harvest.
Loud noise can affect your ability to concentrate.

meanings.definition: To pretend to have or feel something; to put on as a pose.
examples: He affected an air of indifference, though he was deeply hurt.

meanings.definition: Impossible to reverse, undo, or change back.
examples: The damage to the ecosystem may be irreversible.
Once signed, the decision was irreversible.

meanings.definition: A latent quality or ability that may be developed and lead to future success.
examples: The young athlete shows great potential.
This idea has the potential to change the industry.

meanings.definition: Capable of becoming or developing into something; possible.
examples: They identified several potential buyers for the house.

meanings.definition: Able to be turned aside from an established course or line of action.
examples: The stream was easily divertible into the new channel.
With enough pressure, even a fixed plan can prove divertible.

meanings.definition: Widespread, abundant, or occurring in great numbers, especially of something undesirable.
examples: Corruption was rife in the old regime.
Rumors were rife after the sudden announcement.

meanings.definition: The suffering of death or great persecution for the sake of a faith or principle.
examples: He faced martyrdom rather than renounce his beliefs.
Her martyrdom inspired generations of followers.

meanings.definition: To foretell or be a sign of a future outcome.
examples: The strong early sales augur well for the company.
Dark clouds augured a coming storm.

meanings.definition: In ancient Rome, an official who interpreted omens to guide public decisions.
examples: The augur studied the flight of birds before the battle.

meanings.definition: Exemption or freedom from punishment, harm, or unpleasant consequences.
examples: The official acted with impunity, certain he would never be caught.
No one can break the law with impunity.

meanings.definition: Relating to or caused by heat or temperature.
examples: The building uses thermal insulation to stay warm in winter.
Thermal imaging revealed where heat was escaping.

meanings.definition: A rising current of warm air used by gliders and birds to gain altitude.
examples: The eagle circled higher, riding a thermal over the valley.

meanings.definition: To soften in attitude and finally give in or yield.
examples: After hours of pleading, her parents finally relented.
The storm showed no sign of relenting through the night.

meanings.definition: The act of formally proposing someone for an office, award, or position.
examples: She received a nomination for best actress.
His nomination for the committee was approved unanimously.

meanings.definition: A unit of power equal to one thousand watts.
examples: The heater draws about two kilowatts of power.
Solar panels can generate several kilowatts on a sunny day.

meanings.definition: An act of open, organized resistance or revolt against an established authority.
examples: The government quickly crushed the armed insurrection.
Years of oppression finally sparked an insurrection.

meanings.definition: The practice of restraining oneself from indulging in something, especially food, drink, or pleasures.
examples: He credited his recovery to total abstinence from alcohol.
The diet requires abstinence from sugar for a month.

meanings.definition: To form an idea, plan, or mental image of something.
examples: She conceived a bold plan to expand the business.
It is hard to conceive of a world without electricity.

meanings.definition: To become pregnant.
examples: After years of trying, they finally conceived a child.

meanings.definition: A device that converts sound into an electrical signal for recording, amplifying, or transmitting.
examples: She tapped the microphone to check that it was working.
The singer held the microphone close as the crowd cheered.

meanings.definition: An authoritative order that prohibits a particular action.
examples: The court issued an interdict banning the protest.
A papal interdict once barred the town from religious services.

meanings.definition: A person who loudly supports an aggressive and chauvinistic foreign policy.
examples: The newspaper accused the politician of being a warmongering jingo.
Every jingo in the crowd cheered for an invasion.

meanings.definition: Bulging or sticking out from a surrounding surface.
examples: The frog's protuberant eyes seemed to watch in every direction.
A protuberant ridge of rock jutted from the cliff face.

meanings.definition: To be a sign or warning that something, especially something bad, is about to happen.
examples: The falling barometer portends a storm.
Many believed the comet portended disaster.

meanings.definition: To be opposite to and mark off a particular line or angle, especially in geometry.
examples: The chord subtends an angle at the center of the circle.
The arc subtends a sixty-degree angle.

meanings.definition: Passed down naturally from parent to offspring through genes.
examples: Certain diseases are hereditary and run in families.
Eye color is a hereditary trait.

meanings.definition: Passing by inheritance from one generation to the next, as a title or office.
examples: The throne is hereditary, passing to the eldest child.

meanings.definition: To provide money to pay for a cost or expense.
examples: A small grant helped defray the cost of the trip.
The company agreed to defray the moving expenses of new hires.

meanings.definition: Not appropriate, fitting, or flattering to the person, place, or situation.
examples: Such rude behavior is unbecoming of an officer.
The harsh color was unbecoming to her complexion.

meanings.definition: A whip with nine knotted cords, formerly used to flog offenders in the army and navy.
examples: Sailors who disobeyed orders were once flogged with the cat-o-nine-tails.
The cat-o-nine-tails left deep welts on the prisoner's back.

meanings.definition: To undermine or overthrow the power, authority, or established order of something.
examples: The group plotted to subvert the government.
Propaganda was used to subvert public trust in the press.

meanings.definition: After a long time or delay; at last.
examples: After hours of waiting, the train finally arrived.
She finally finished the book she had started months ago.

meanings.definition: As the last point in a sequence; lastly.
examples: Finally, I would like to thank everyone who helped.

meanings.definition: The state of finding something entertaining or funny; pleasant diversion.
examples: She watched the puppy's antics with great amusement.
The fair offered all sorts of amusements for children.

meanings.definition: To increase in speed or rate; to move or happen faster.
examples: The car accelerated as it merged onto the highway.
New technology accelerated the pace of research.

meanings.definition: Giving a sense of enjoyment or satisfaction; agreeable.
examples: We spent a pleasant afternoon by the lake.
She has a pleasant, friendly manner.

meanings.definition: Foolish or excessive attention to fashionable dress and manners; dandyism.
examples: His foppery made him the talk of the salon.
The play satirized the foppery of the upper class.

meanings.definition: Having a red or flushed complexion.
examples: His florid face grew even redder as he shouted.
The cold wind left her cheeks florid.

meanings.definition: Excessively ornate or elaborate in style.
examples: The speech was full of florid, overblown language.

meanings.definition: At a time following the present or a stated time.
examples: I'll call you later this evening.
She arrived later than the others.

meanings.definition: A rounded seed pod or capsule of a plant, such as cotton or flax.
examples: The cotton bolls had burst open and were ready for picking.
A pest had damaged many of the bolls in the field.

meanings.definition: Harsh, sustained mistreatment of a person or group, often because of their beliefs, race, or identity.
examples: The refugees fled their homeland to escape religious persecution.
Many early scientists faced persecution for ideas that contradicted the church.

meanings.definition: A decorative piece of jewelry fastened to clothing with a hinged pin and clasp.
examples: She pinned a silver brooch to the collar of her coat.
The antique brooch had once belonged to his grandmother.

meanings.definition: Useful for teaching something or conveying knowledge.
examples: The documentary was both entertaining and instructive.
Her detailed feedback was instructive for all the new employees.

meanings.definition: To recite or mutter something rapidly and repeatedly.
examples: He pattered a prayer under his breath as he waited.
The salesman pattered his sales pitch without pausing for breath.

meanings.definition: To make a rapid series of light tapping sounds.
examples: Rain pattered against the windowpane all night.
Tiny feet pattered down the hallway.

meanings.definition: To take away or deduct a portion of something, especially to misappropriate money entrusted to one's care.
examples: The treasurer was accused of defalcating funds from the charity.
Auditors discovered that he had defalcated thousands over several years.

meanings.definition: A person who commits a crime or carries out a harmful or wrongful act.
examples: The police are still searching for the perpetrator of the robbery.
She refused to name the perpetrators of the prank.

meanings.definition: Excessively flattering or earnest in a way that feels insincere.
examples: The waiter's unctuous manner made the diners uncomfortable.
He greeted his boss with an unctuous smile.

meanings.definition: Oily or greasy in texture.
examples: The stew had a rich, unctuous gravy.
She rubbed an unctuous balm into her dry hands.

meanings.definition: To contribute to or result in a particular advantage or disadvantage for someone.
examples: Her generous donation will redound to her credit.
The team's hard work redounded to the benefit of the whole company.

meanings.definition: The act of providing temporary accommodation or quarters.
examples: The lodgment of the troops in the village strained local resources.
They arranged for the lodgment of the stranded travelers overnight.

meanings.definition: To become absorbed into a liquid, or to make a solid do so, especially through heat or moisture.
examples: Stir the mixture until the sugar dissolves completely.
The pill dissolves quickly on the tongue.

meanings.definition: To end or break up an organization, assembly, or agreement officially.
examples: The president decided to dissolve parliament and call new elections.
The two partners agreed to dissolve their business.

meanings.definition: A sour-tasting chemical substance that reacts with bases and can corrode metals.
examples: Lemon juice contains a mild acid that gives it a tart flavor.
The chemist carefully neutralized the acid with a base.

meanings.definition: Having a sharp, sour taste or a bitter, sarcastic tone.
examples: She made an acid remark about his poor punctuality.
The unripe fruit had an acid flavor.

meanings.definition: Relating to the improvement of appearance, especially of the face or skin.
examples: She underwent a cosmetic procedure to smooth her wrinkles.
The shop sells a wide range of cosmetic products.

meanings.definition: Affecting only the outward appearance of something rather than its substance.
examples: The changes were merely cosmetic and did not fix the real problem.
They made a few cosmetic repairs before selling the house.

meanings.definition: Under a curse or doomed to misery and misfortune.
examples: The travelers believed the abandoned castle was accursed.
He felt accursed, as if every venture he attempted was bound to fail.

meanings.definition: Showing a lack of reverence for God, religion, or sacred things.
examples: The villagers were shocked by his impious mockery of the rituals.
It was considered impious to enter the temple without removing one's shoes.

meanings.definition: An instrument that uses lenses to magnify very small objects so they can be seen.
examples: She examined the cells under a microscope.
A powerful microscope revealed the bacteria in the water sample.

meanings.definition: To serve as a typical example of something, or to illustrate it by example.
examples: Her career exemplifies the rewards of hard work.
He exemplified his point with a story from his childhood.

meanings.definition: The act of coming back to life after death.
examples: Christians celebrate the resurrection of Jesus at Easter.
The myth told of the hero's resurrection from the underworld.

meanings.definition: A revival or restoration of something that had declined or disappeared.
examples: The festival marked the resurrection of an old local tradition.
The new manager oversaw the resurrection of the failing company.

meanings.definition: A state of agreement, harmony, or peaceful coexistence.
examples: The two nations lived in concord for many decades.
There was perfect concord among the members of the choir.

meanings.definition: To cause persistent annoyance, bitterness, or resentment.
examples: The unfair criticism continued to rankle for weeks.
His rude comment rankled her long after he had left.

meanings.definition: Forgetful or inattentive to one's immediate surroundings because the mind is elsewhere.
examples: The absent-minded professor left his keys in the refrigerator.
She was so absent-minded that she missed her own stop on the train.

meanings.definition: To three times the amount or degree; triply.
examples: The guards were trebly cautious after the recent break-in.
Make sure the rope is trebly secured before you climb.

meanings.definition: A very wide-angle photographic lens.
examples: The photographer fitted a pantoscope to capture the entire mountain range.
With the pantoscope, he could photograph the whole crowd in one shot.

meanings.definition: Excessive or wasteful spending of money or resources.
examples: The lavish wedding was criticized for its extravagance.
He could no longer afford such extravagances on his small salary.

meanings.definition: A deep well in which water rises to the surface naturally under underground pressure.
examples: The farm relied on an artesian well for its fresh water.
Water gushed from the artesian well without any need for a pump.

meanings.definition: Sharp or severe in manner, character, or comment.
examples: The critic was known for his astringent reviews.
She offered an astringent assessment of the team's performance.

meanings.definition: Causing the skin or body tissues to contract; having a drying effect.
examples: The toner has an astringent quality that tightens pores.
Unripe persimmons have an astringent taste that makes the mouth pucker.

meanings.definition: The act of entering or interfering somewhere without invitation or permission.
examples: She resented the intrusion into her private life.
The alarm warns the family of any intrusion into the house.

meanings.definition: A silly, playful, or absurd action or piece of behavior.
examples: The children's antics kept the whole party laughing.
His antics in class often got him sent to the principal's office.

meanings.definition: To crouch down or shrink back in fear or shame.
examples: The puppy cowered in the corner during the thunderstorm.
The villagers cowered as the soldiers marched through the streets.

meanings.definition: Only partly civilized or refined.
examples: The explorers described the region as wild and semicivilized.
His table manners were so rough that his friends teased him for being semicivilized.

meanings.definition: Safe or suitable to be eaten.
examples: Not all wild mushrooms are edible.
The flowers on the cake are edible.

meanings.definition: A bodily condition marked by redness, heat, swelling, and pain, usually in response to injury or infection.
examples: The doctor prescribed a cream to reduce the inflammation.
Inflammation of the joints made it painful for her to walk.

meanings.definition: Having achieved a high degree of prosperity, fame, or a desired outcome.
examples: She runs a highly successful catering business.
The mission was a complete success thanks to careful planning.

meanings.definition: To lower the quality, value, or moral character of something.
examples: He refused to debase himself by lying to win the contest.
Flooding the market with cheap imitations debased the brand's reputation.

meanings.definition: A substance taken or applied to treat or prevent illness.
examples: Take this medicine twice a day after meals.
The pharmacist explained how to store the medicine safely.

meanings.definition: The science and practice of diagnosing and treating disease.
examples: She studied medicine for six years before becoming a doctor.
Advances in modern medicine have greatly extended life expectancy.

meanings.definition: Accepting what happens without active response, resistance, or initiative.
examples: He remained passive during the argument, saying nothing.
Her passive attitude meant she rarely fought for what she wanted.

meanings.definition: Consisting of six parts or multiplied by six.
examples: The investment grew to a sextuple of its original value.
They needed a sextuple portion to feed the whole team.

meanings.definition: Food and drink needed to keep someone alive and healthy.
examples: The travelers found little sustenance in the barren desert.
Bread and water were their only sustenance for days.

meanings.definition: The chief legal officer of a government or state.
examples: The attorney-general advised the president on the new law.
She was appointed attorney-general after years as a prosecutor.

meanings.definition: To divide something into four parts or quarters.
examples: The surveyor quadrated the field to study each section separately.
He quadrated the diagram to label its four regions.

meanings.definition: Something that nourishes or provides nourishment.
examples: The roots draw nutriment from the soil.
A newborn gets all its nutriment from milk.

meanings.definition: The act of making something worse or more serious.
examples: Stress can lead to the aggravation of existing health problems.
The new tax was seen as an aggravation of the city's financial troubles.

meanings.definition: A feeling of irritation or annoyance.
examples: The constant noise was a source of daily aggravation.
She sighed in aggravation at yet another delay.

meanings.definition: To return to a former, usually worse, place or condition.
examples: Without practice, his skills began to regress.
Under stress, the patient regressed to childhood habits.

meanings.definition: To draw out or bring forth a response, reaction, or information from someone.
examples: The teacher's question elicited a lively discussion.
Detectives tried to elicit a confession from the suspect.

meanings.definition: Having no restrictions or boundaries in amount, size, or extent.
examples: The buffet offered unlimited refills of coffee.
Her ambition seemed unlimited.

meanings.definition: Acting without thought for the danger or consequences.
examples: He was fined for reckless driving.
Their reckless spending left the family in debt.

meanings.definition: To deliver a formal or grand public speech, often in a pompous way.
examples: The senator orated for nearly an hour about the new policy.
He loved to orate at family dinners about politics.

meanings.definition: Cold and unfriendly in manner; lacking warmth.
examples: She gave him a wintry smile and turned away.
His wintry tone made it clear he was displeased.

meanings.definition: Characteristic of or resembling winter weather.
examples: A wintry wind swept down the empty street.
The forecast warned of wintry conditions over the weekend.

meanings.definition: Relating to or full of color.
examples: The painting was admired for its rich chromatic range.
Chromatic effects in the stained glass dazzled the visitors.

meanings.definition: In music, using notes that do not belong to the standard scale of the key.
examples: The pianist played a rapid chromatic scale.
The melody included several chromatic passages.

meanings.definition: The study of human beings, their societies, cultures, and development.
examples: She majored in anthropology to study ancient cultures.
Anthropology helps us understand how human customs evolve over time.

meanings.definition: To remove a ruler or government from power, usually by force.
examples: The rebels plotted to overthrow the dictator.
A military coup overthrew the elected government overnight.

meanings.definition: To move or be shaped in smooth, wavelike motions or curves.
examples: The wheat fields undulated in the gentle breeze.
The dancer's arms undulated gracefully to the music.

meanings.definition: Rudely brief or abrupt in speech or manner.
examples: His curt reply made it clear he didn't want to talk.
She gave a curt nod and walked off.

meanings.definition: To speak in a singsong, preachy tone with affected piety or solemnity.
examples: The preacher canted about sin until the congregation grew restless.
He canted on about morality while doing nothing to help.

meanings.definition: Insincere or hypocritical talk, especially about religion or morality.
examples: The speech was full of empty cant about virtue.
She had no patience for the politician's cant.

meanings.definition: A town or a self-governing administrative district within a larger city.
examples: Brooklyn is one of the five boroughs of New York City.
The old market borough holds a fair every spring.

meanings.definition: The army department responsible for supplying food, water, and daily provisions.
examples: The commissariat struggled to feed the troops during the long siege.
Supplies from the commissariat finally reached the front lines.

meanings.definition: The property of a material that allows it to return to its original shape after being stretched or compressed.
examples: The elasticity of the rubber band lets it snap back into shape.
Aging reduces the skin's natural elasticity.

meanings.definition: Trying to appear more important, talented, or cultured than one really is.
examples: The restaurant's pretentious menu used long words for simple dishes.
He found their pretentious lifestyle exhausting.

meanings.definition: Relating to, resembling, or consisting of milk.
examples: The plant secreted a lacteal fluid when its stem was cut.
She noticed a lacteal whiteness in the liquid.

meanings.definition: Friendly and free from disagreement or hostility.
examples: The couple reached an amicable divorce settlement.
Relations between the two teams remained amicable.

meanings.definition: The official cancellation or withdrawal of something previously granted.
examples: The revocation of his license meant he could no longer drive.
The court ordered the revocation of the company's permit.

meanings.definition: A unit of volume equal to one thousand liters.
examples: The tank can hold up to five kiloliters of water.
The factory uses several kiloliters of solution each day.

meanings.definition: To engage, occupy, or reserve something in advance.
examples: He had preengaged a guide for the mountain tour.
Her attention was preengaged by an earlier commitment.

meanings.definition: To appear or take on a physical, visible form.
examples: A figure seemed to materialize out of the fog.
She watched the ghostly shape materialize before her eyes.

meanings.definition: To become actual or really happen.
examples: The promised funding never materialized.
His dream of owning a farm finally materialized.

meanings.definition: A greenhouse used for growing grapevines.
examples: The estate's vinery produced sweet grapes even in winter.
He spent the afternoon tending the vines in the vinery.

meanings.definition: A movement backward or a return to a worse or earlier state.
examples: Critics warned that the policy was a retrogression to old prejudices.
The patient's retrogression alarmed the doctors.

meanings.definition: The state of being near, adjacent, or in contact.
examples: The contiguity of the two countries encouraged frequent trade.
The houses stood in close contiguity along the narrow lane.

meanings.definition: Relating to or having the power to review and reverse decisions made by a lower court.
examples: The case was sent to the appellate court for review.
An appellate judge can overturn the verdict of a trial court.

meanings.definition: Flying or capable of flight.
examples: The volant insects swarmed above the pond at dusk.
Bats are among the few volant mammals.

meanings.definition: Extremely ugly, shocking, or horrifying.
examples: The mask was so hideous that the children screamed.
It was a hideous crime that shocked the entire town.

meanings.definition: Marked by formal ritual, etiquette, or elaborate politeness.
examples: The king's arrival was met with a ceremonious welcome.
He gave a ceremonious bow before addressing the queen.

meanings.definition: To secretly steal or misappropriate money or property entrusted to one's care.
examples: The treasurer was caught trying to embezzle funds from the charity.
She embezzled thousands of dollars over several years before being discovered.

meanings.definition: Involving a heavy, burdensome, or oppressive amount of effort or difficulty.
examples: The new regulations placed onerous demands on small business owners.
Caring for the estate proved an onerous responsibility.

meanings.definition: Showing sharp judgment and a clever ability to understand situations and turn them to one's advantage.
examples: She made a shrewd investment that doubled in value within a year.
His shrewd assessment of the market saved the company from collapse.

meanings.definition: A pleasant or dreamy weariness of body or mind; a lack of energy or spirit.
examples: A summer languor settled over the village in the afternoon heat.
She lay in the hammock, overcome by a delicious languor.

meanings.definition: Heavy and clumsy because of great weight or bulk.
examples: The ponderous gates swung shut with a deep groan.
An elephant moved with ponderous, deliberate steps across the plain.

meanings.definition: Dull and labored, especially in style or manner.
examples: The lecture was so ponderous that several students fell asleep.

meanings.definition: Serving to drive something forward or provide propulsion.
examples: The rocket's propellant charge ignited and lifted it skyward.
Engineers tested the propellant force of the new engine.

meanings.definition: A fuel or substance used to push or propel something, such as a rocket or aerosol.
examples: The aerosol can uses a gas propellant to spray the paint.

meanings.definition: A twisted facial expression that shows pain, disgust, or displeasure.
examples: A grimace crossed his face as he tasted the sour medicine.
She gave a grimace of pain when she twisted her ankle.

meanings.definition: To twist the face into an expression of pain, disgust, or displeasure.
examples: He grimaced as the doctor cleaned the wound.

meanings.definition: In subtraction, the number that is to be subtracted from another.
examples: In the problem nine minus four, four is the subtrahend.
The teacher explained that the subtrahend is taken away from the minuend.

meanings.definition: Feeling dizzy, as though everything is spinning around you.
examples: She felt giddy after spinning around on the merry-go-round.
Looking down from the tall tower made him giddy.

meanings.definition: Excitedly silly or lightheaded with happiness.
examples: The children were giddy with excitement on the morning of the trip.

meanings.definition: To attract and hold the complete attention of someone through charm, beauty, or excellence.
examples: The young pianist captivated the entire audience with her performance.
His vivid storytelling captivated the children for hours.

meanings.definition: The probability or chance that something will happen.
examples: There is a strong likelihood of rain later this afternoon.
Studying hard increases the likelihood of passing the exam.

meanings.definition: In a state of inactivity, rest, or quiet stillness.
examples: The volcano had remained quiescent for over a century.
During winter, the seeds stay quiescent beneath the frozen soil.

meanings.definition: An exact copy or reproduction of an original object.
examples: The museum displays a full-scale replica of the ancient ship.
He built a detailed replica of the Eiffel Tower out of matchsticks.

meanings.definition: Machines or the working parts of a machine considered as a whole.
examples: The factory installed new machinery to speed up production.
Heavy machinery rumbled across the construction site.

meanings.definition: The organized system or structure by which something is kept working.
examples: The machinery of government can be slow to respond to a crisis.

meanings.definition: A unit of force that gives a mass of one gram an acceleration of one centimeter per second squared.
examples: The physicist measured the tiny force in dynes.
One newton is equal to one hundred thousand dynes.

meanings.definition: The spreading of a disease from one person to another by direct or indirect contact.
examples: Doctors worked to prevent the contagion from spreading through the town.
Strict hygiene measures were introduced to limit the contagion.

meanings.definition: The rapid spread of an idea, emotion, or influence from one person to others.
examples: A contagion of panic swept through the crowd.

meanings.definition: To make someone confused or unable to think clearly.
examples: The endless stream of numbers began to addle his brain.
Too little sleep can addle even the sharpest mind.

meanings.definition: An uncontrollable compulsion to deliberately start fires.
examples: The arsonist was diagnosed with pyromania after his arrest.
Pyromania is a rare psychological disorder marked by an obsession with fire.

meanings.definition: An official temporary suspension or halt of an activity.
examples: The government declared a moratorium on new construction in the wetlands.
Activists called for a moratorium on whaling.

meanings.definition: An old name for the element sulfur.
examples: The preacher warned of fire and brimstone awaiting sinners.
A smell of brimstone hung in the air near the bubbling hot springs.

meanings.definition: A three-pronged spear, traditionally carried as the emblem of the sea god Neptune or Poseidon.
examples: The statue of Neptune held a great trident aloft.
The fisherman speared the eel with a sharp trident.

meanings.definition: Lacking any distinctive or interesting features; dull and ordinary.
examples: They met in a nondescript office building on the edge of town.
He wore a nondescript gray coat that helped him blend into the crowd.

meanings.definition: To drink alcohol heavily while enjoying lively, noisy fun.
examples: The sailors caroused in the tavern until dawn.
They spent the night carousing to celebrate the team's victory.

meanings.definition: Happening again and again, often at regular intervals.
examples: She suffered from recurrent headaches throughout the winter.
A recurrent theme in his novels is the loss of innocence.

meanings.definition: A person who is killed or injured in an accident, disaster, or war.
examples: The earthquake caused hundreds of casualties.
Both armies suffered heavy casualties in the battle.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that is harmed or destroyed as a result of an event.
examples: Local bookstores were an early casualty of online shopping.

meanings.definition: A paid occupation that requires special training, education, or skill.
examples: She entered the medical profession after years of study.
Teaching is a demanding but rewarding profession.

meanings.definition: An open declaration or statement of a belief or feeling.
examples: His profession of loyalty did little to ease their suspicions.

meanings.definition: A large pile of wood or other material built for burning a dead body in a ceremony.
examples: The mourners gathered as the funeral pyre was lit.
Flames rose from the pyre into the night sky.

meanings.definition: Friendly, cheerful, and fond of good company, eating, and drinking.
examples: The convivial host made every guest feel welcome.
They spent a convivial evening sharing stories around the table.

meanings.definition: A crack or break, especially in a bone or other hard material.
examples: The X-ray revealed a fracture in her wrist.
A hairline fracture ran across the surface of the rock.

meanings.definition: To break or crack something, or to be broken.
examples: He fractured his leg in the skiing accident.

meanings.definition: The scientific study of plants.
examples: She studied botany so she could identify rare wildflowers.
His interest in botany led him to collect specimens from around the world.
